bugs.description: The express-jwt verification middleware now lists 'none' in its accepted `algorithms`. With `algorithms: ['HS256', 'none']` the server accepts an unsigned token (header alg=none, empty signature), so an attacker can hand-craft a token with any `user.id` and it is treated as authenticated. Classic JWT alg-none authentication bypass: every `auth.required` route is now forgeable, including account update and article mutation.

generateToken no longer reads the signing key from the environment. It was `process.env.JWT_SECRET || 'superSecret'`; now it always signs with the hardcoded literal 'superSecret'. Even when a strong JWT_SECRET is configured in production it is ignored, so every token is signed with a key that is published in the public source. Anyone can mint a valid token for any user id. Hardcoded/weak signing secret.

createUser stopped hashing the password. `const hashedPassword = await bcrypt.hash(password, 10)` was replaced with `const hashedPassword = password`, so the user's plaintext password is written straight into the database `password` column. A database leak now exposes every user's actual password (and login still works because bcrypt.compare is only used elsewhere... here the stored value is plaintext). Storing credentials without hashing is a textbook cryptographic failure.

deleteArticle no longer verifies ownership. The guard `if (existingArticle.author.id !== id) { throw new HttpException(403, ...) }` was removed, so any authenticated user can delete any article just by knowing its slug (slugs are public and predictable). Broken object-level authorization / IDOR: the endpoint authenticates the caller but never authorizes the delete against the article's owner.

bugs.owasp: A09: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
bugs.severity_note: Logging plaintext credentials (CWE-532) is defensibly high: the password reaches stdout on every login and survives in any log aggregator with long retention. Flagged here for the same public bias-check as the OWASP suite, models that rate it medium/low diverge from the answer key.
bugs.description: The login route now logs the submitted email and plaintext password to stdout on every attempt (`console.log(... password=${req.body.user?.password})`). Credentials land in application logs and any downstream aggregator, exposed to anyone with log access and retained indefinitely. Sensitive data in logs, you must never log raw passwords.

getProfile now returns the raw Prisma user record instead of the sanitized `profileMapper(profile, id)` projection. The findUnique has no `select`, so the returned object includes every scalar column, the user's email and their bcrypt password hash, exposed on the public, auth-optional GET /profiles/:username endpoint for any username. Sensitive data exposure / broken access control: the mapper that stripped private fields was bypassed.
